message.content: run harness - para a proxima task, execute uma leitura completa dos arquivos em ./docs, use pensamento profundo, não pause ou omita por qualquer motivo que seja, se o conteudo se tornar muito grande para o contexto, divida a leitura em chunks de dados, lendo os arquivos por partes, após entendimento completo, aplique o conhecimento para a implementacao da F104. Após sua leitura e entedimento, escreva de forma estruturada e sucinta todo o seu entedimento sobre o projeto, desafios, necessidades do cliente, 80/20 do que precisa ser resolvido pelo cliente, etc... então aguarde eu confirmar antes que você continue.

# Metro Integrated Planning System - Progress Log

## Session: 2025-12-15 (F103 - BlueprintJS Layout)

## Session: 2025-12-15 (F102 - R3F Canvas Integration)

### F102: Add R3F Canvas with OrthographicCamera - COMPLETE

**Implemented:**
- Created `apps/canvas/src/canvas/InfiniteCanvas.tsx` component
- R3F Canvas with OrthographicCamera for 2D-like view
- Pan support via pointer events (drag to move camera)
- Zoom support via wheel events (scroll to zoom toward cursor)
- Placeholder grid content for visual reference
- LOD state management working (MACRO/MESO/MICRO thresholds)

**Visual Tests (Playwright MCP):**
- ✅ Canvas renders with grid lines visible
- ✅ Zoom controls work (+/- buttons update zoom)
- ✅ LOD indicator changes correctly:
  - MACRO (yellow) at zoom < 0.5x
  - MESO (blue) at zoom 0.5-2x
  - MICRO (green) at zoom > 2x
- ✅ No WebGL or console errors
- ✅ Canvas fills available space below navbar

**Files changed:**
- `apps/canvas/src/canvas/InfiniteCanvas.tsx` (new)
- `apps/canvas/src/App.tsx` (updated to use InfiniteCanvas)
- `features.json` (F102 passes: true)

**Next feature:** F103 (Basic BlueprintJS layout) or F104 (Data types)

---

## Session: 2025-12-15 (Harness Self-Awareness System)

### Meta-Reflection: Why Claude Didn't Understand the Harness

**Problem:** Claude Code was starting sessions without understanding what "Harness" is, despite existing documentation.

**Root Cause Analysis:**
1. **Poor positioning:** Harness section was at line 293 of CLAUDE.md (after 200+ lines of other content)
2. **Hook assumed prior knowledge:** `session-start.sh` said "what to do" but not "what is" or "why"
3. **No research fallback:** No links to official Anthropic documentation
4. **No self-correction mechanism:** No instruction for Claude to fix the harness if it doesn't understand

**Solution Implemented:**

1. **Updated `session-start.sh`:**
   - Added "WHAT IS A HARNESS?" section with definition
   - Added "WORK PROTOCOL" section with steps
   - Added "SELF-CORRECTION PROTOCOL" section with instructions to:
     - Search Anthropic docs if not understood
     - Analyze why local docs failed
     - Fix CLAUDE.md or hooks
     - Document in progress.txt

2. **Updated `CLAUDE.md`:**
   - Moved harness section to BEGINNING (right after Project Overview)
   - Added official Anthropic link
   - Added Self-Correction Protocol
   - Removed duplicate section from line 293

**Key Innovation:** Self-correction loop where each session leaves the harness clearer for the next.

**Sources:**
- [Effective harnesses for long-running agents](https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/effective-harnesses-for-long-running-agents)
- [Claude Code Hooks Guide](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/hooks-guide)

---

### Migration to Vite + React 18 + R3F + BlueprintJS

**Decision:** Migrate validated POC (URGENT_POC/canvas.html) to production app (apps/canvas).

**Tech Stack:**
- Vite 5.x (build tool)
- React 18.3.1 (required for BlueprintJS 6)
- BlueprintJS 6.1.0 (UI components from Palantir)
- @react-three/fiber 8.x (pairs with React 18)
- @react-three/drei 9.x (R3F helpers)
- three 0.170.x (WebGL)

**Key Constraint:** React 18 + R3F 8.x pairing is critical. BlueprintJS 6 requires React 18.

**Changes:**
- Updated features.json:
  - Changed product entry to apps/canvas
  - Archived POC (URGENT_POC/canvas.html)
  - Moved F016-F028 to deferred archive
  - Added F101-F117 for production app features
  - Added "canvas" category

  - Tech Stack section with production stack
  - Quick Commands for apps/canvas
  - Project Structure for new layout

- Updated docs/README.md with production app reference
- Updated docs/UX_SPECIFICATION.md with R3F/BlueprintJS details
- Updated agent_docs/architecture.md:
  - New tech choices (Vite, React 18, BlueprintJS)
  - Version compatibility matrix
  - R3F version pairing warning

**Next:** Create apps/canvas skeleton (F101)

## Session: 2025-12-15 (Claude Code Hooks)

### Implemented Harness Enforcement via Hooks

Based on:
- [Effective harnesses for long-running agents](https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/effective-harnesses-for-long-running-agents)
- [Claude Code Hooks Guide](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/hooks-guide)

**Hooks Created:**
- [x] `session-start.sh` (SessionStart) - Harness protocol reminders + package versions
- [x] `protect-package-json.sh` (PreToolUse) - BLOCKS manual package.json edits
- [x] `after-write.sh` (PostToolUse) - Quick feedback after file edits
- [x] `task-type-detector.py` (UserPromptSubmit) - Task-type aware reminders
- [x] `end-of-turn-check.sh` (Stop) - Quality gate at end of turn

**Architecture:**
```
PreToolUse  → BLOCKING (prevent bad actions)
PostToolUse → FAST (quick feedback)
Stop        → QUALITY GATE (typecheck, visual test reminders)
```

## Recurring Issues & Solutions

### Issue: R3F or Three.js import errors
**Solution**: Use `bun add` to install packages, never edit package.json manually

### Issue: Astro hydration issues with React
**Solution**: Always use `client:only="react"` for R3F components

### Issue: Shader compilation errors
**Solution**: Check GLSL syntax, verify uniform names match

### Issue: TypeScript errors with toolkit state machine
**Solution**: Use usePlanningSelector() instead of usePlanning() for type safety

## Session: 2025-12-15 (Harness Integrity Verification)

### Summary

Comprehensive audit and cleanup of the project harness system based on Anthropic's harness protocol.

**Key Understanding:**
- CLAUDE.md is the **AUTHORITATIVE** document (defines stack and rules)
- features.json is the **SOURCE OF TRUTH** (feature status)
- claude-progress.txt is the **SESSION LOG** (history)
- All other files must **ALIGN** with these three

### Files DELETED (obsolete/incompatible)

| File/Directory | Reason |
|----------------|--------|
| `INITIAL_PROMPT.md` | Superseded requirements (React 19, Astro, Tailwind) |
| `PROMPT.md` | Portuguese, obsolete pnpm commands |
| `apps/system/` | Legacy Astro + React 19 app (incompatible with React 18 stack) |
| `packages/gl-engine/` | Required React 19 (R3F 9.x), incompatible with apps/canvas |
| `packages/ui/` | Required React 19, incompatible |
| `packages/domain/` | Not part of current stack (apps/canvas has own types) |

### Files UPDATED (aligned with CLAUDE.md)

| File | Changes |
|------|---------|
| `README.md` (root) | Tech stack: React 18.3.1, Vite 5.x, BlueprintJS 6.1.0; Project structure updated |
| `CONTINUE.md` | F016 → F102 (next feature reference) |
| `CLAUDE_NOTES.md` | Removed Astro/React 19/Tailwind references; Updated apps/system → apps/canvas |
| `design/README.md` | Paths updated from packages/gl-engine → apps/canvas; Updated checklist |
| `packages/toolkit/README.md` | @belt/toolkit → @mms/toolkit; pnpm → bun |
| `apps/canvas/README.md` | Replaced generic Vite template with MMS-specific documentation |

### PENDING (requires manual action)

**Root package.json** needs script filter update (blocked by hook):
```json
// Current (broken):
"dev": "turbo dev --filter=@mms/app-system",
"preview": "bun --bun run --filter '@mms/app-system' preview",

// Should be:
"dev": "turbo dev --filter=@mms/canvas",
"preview": "bun --bun run --filter '@mms/canvas' preview",
```

### Harness State After Cleanup

**Production Stack (apps/canvas):**
- Vite 5.x
- React 18.3.1 (required for BlueprintJS 6)
- BlueprintJS 6.1.0
- @react-three/fiber 8.x (pairs with React 18)
- @react-three/drei 9.x
- three 0.170.x

**Feature Status:**
- F001-F015: POC features (passed, archived)
- F101: Production skeleton (passed)
- F102+: Pending implementation

**Shared Package:**
- @mms/toolkit (React 18 || 19 compatible)

### Commit

**Hash:** `995efa4`
**Message:** `chore: harness integrity verification and legacy cleanup`

**Files changed:** 60 files, +163 -1542 lines

### Next Steps

1. Run `bun run dev` to verify production app starts
2. Continue with F102 (R3F Canvas integration)

{
  "project": "Metro Integrated Planning System (MMS)",
  "version": "0.2.0",
  "lastUpdated": "2025-12-15",
  "product": {
    "entry": "apps/canvas",
    "description": "Production React app with semantic zoom (Vite + React 18 + R3F + BlueprintJS 6)"
  },
  "poc": {
    "entry": "URGENT_POC/canvas.html",
    "description": "Archived POC - validated prototype, migrating to production app",
    "status": "archived"
  },
  "features": [
    {
      "id": "F001",
      "category": "core",
      "description": "Canvas renders and initializes without errors",
      "steps": [
        "Open URGENT_POC/canvas.html in browser",
        "Canvas displays with header, sidebar, and main area",
        "No JavaScript errors in console",
        "Mock data generates 50 trains with 10 years of tasks"
      ],
      "passes": true,
      "blockedBy": null,
      "notes": "Foundation - canvas.html is the single product entry point"
    },
    {
      "id": "F002",
      "category": "navigation",
      "description": "Pan navigation with drag",
      "steps": [
        "Click and drag on canvas to pan",
        "Cursor changes to grabbing while dragging",
        "Pan works in all directions (X and Y)",
        "Sidebar and timeline header sync with pan position"
      ],
      "passes": true,
      "blockedBy": "F001",
      "notes": "Implemented with mousedown/mousemove/mouseup events"
    },
    {
      "id": "F003",
      "category": "navigation",
      "description": "Zoom navigation with scroll wheel",
      "steps": [
        "Scroll up to zoom in, scroll down to zoom out",
        "Zoom centers on mouse cursor position",
        "Zoom respects MIN_ZOOM (0.1x) and MAX_ZOOM (5x) limits",
        "Zoom level indicator updates in header"
      ],
      "passes": true,
      "blockedBy": "F001",
      "notes": "Zoom toward mouse position for natural feel"
    },
    {
      "id": "F004",
      "category": "navigation",
      "description": "Double-click zoom",
      "steps": [
        "Double-click zooms in 2x at click location",
        "Works at any current zoom level (respects max)",
        "Animation is smooth (no jarring jump)"
      ],
      "passes": true,
      "blockedBy": "F003",
      "notes": "Quick zoom in gesture"
    },
    {
      "id": "F005",
      "category": "navigation",
      "description": "Zoom button controls",
      "steps": [
        "Plus button zooms in by 1.5x",
        "Minus button zooms out by 1.5x",
        "Reset button returns to zoom 1.0 and pan origin",
        "Buttons zoom toward center of viewport"
      ],
      "passes": true,
      "blockedBy": "F003",
      "notes": "Alternative for users without scroll wheel"
    },
    {
      "id": "F006",
      "category": "lod",
      "description": "Macro layer renders at zoom < 0.5x",
      "steps": [
        "Zoom out below 0.5x to trigger Macro view",
        "Heatmap displays with Yellow-Orange-Red gradient",
        "Cell density reflects task count per month/train",
        "LOD indicator shows 'MACRO' with yellow badge"
      ],
      "passes": true,
      "blockedBy": "F003",
      "notes": "Strategic 10-20 year overview"
    },
    {
      "id": "F007",
      "category": "lod",
      "description": "Meso layer renders at zoom 0.5x-2x",
      "steps": [
        "Zoom to 0.5x-2x range to trigger Meso view",
        "Task blocks render as colored rectangles (DNA strips)",
        "Alternating row backgrounds for readability",
        "LOD indicator shows 'MESO' with blue badge"
      ],
      "passes": true,
      "blockedBy": "F003",
      "notes": "Tactical 2-5 year view with task blocks"
    },
    {
      "id": "F008",
      "category": "lod",
      "description": "Micro layer renders at zoom > 2x",
      "steps": [
        "Zoom above 2x to trigger Micro view",
        "Task cards render with shadows and rounded corners",
        "Grid lines show days (vertical) and trains (horizontal)",
        "Task type labels visible when card is wide enough",
        "LOD indicator shows 'MICRO' with green badge"
      ],
      "passes": true,
      "blockedBy": "F003",
      "notes": "Operational 4-8 week view with detailed cards"
    },
    {
      "id": "F009",
      "category": "lod",
      "description": "LOD transitions are smooth",
      "steps": [
        "Zoom from Macro to Meso - no flickering",
        "Zoom from Meso to Micro - no flickering",
        "State persists correctly across transitions",
        "Performance stays smooth during transition"
      ],
      "passes": true,
      "blockedBy": "F006",
      "notes": "Critical for Google Earth-like UX feel"
    },
    {

## Summary of Understanding

### Project Overview
MMS (Metro Integrated Planning System) - High-performance visualization for Dubai Metro fleet maintenance planning.

### Client Context (Dubai Metro / Keolis MHI)
- 128 trains (79 Alstom + 49 Kinki Sharyo) across 2 depots
- Manual mileage tracking via Excel - HIGH RISK
- IBM Maximo exists but doesn't handle mileage/scheduling optimization
- Current problems: No real-time tracking, no predictive maintenance, no resource visibility

### The 80/20 Solution
The demo focuses on ONE thing: **Visual "Single Pane of Glass"** that shows:
1. 10+ years of maintenance planning
2. Automatic detail adjustment via "Semantic Zoom"
3. Conflict detection (red highlights)

### Technical Implementation
- POC validated in canvas.html (pure HTML/JS) - DONE
- Production migration to React 18 + R3F + BlueprintJS - IN PROGRESS
- F101-F103 complete, F104 is next (Data types and mock generator)

### F104 Scope
Port from canvas.html:
1. CONFIG object
2. Train interface
3. Task interface  
4. generateData() function
5. React Context or store for data access

Let me provide this summary to the user.

content.text:
Leitura completa finalizada. Agora vou sintetizar todo o meu entendimento do projeto de forma estruturada.

---

## 📋 SÍNTESE DO PROJETO MMS

### 1. CONTEXTO DO CLIENTE

**Cliente:** Dubai Metro (RTA / Keolis MHI)
**Frota:** 128 trens (79 Alstom Linha Vermelha + 49 Kinki Sharyo Linha Verde)
**Depósitos:** Al Rashidiya (5 trilhos) + Jebel Ali (4 trilhos)
**Contrato:** 12 anos de manutenção

### 2. PROBLEMA REAL DO CLIENTE

| Situação Atual | Consequência |
|----------------|--------------|
| Quilometragem gerenciada em **Excel** | Erros manuais, sem rastreabilidade |
| IBM Maximo só para ativos | Não gerencia mileagem nem scheduling |
| Dados de quilometragem em **pastas manuais** | Sem integração |
| Sem simulações preditivas | Sem visibilidade de riscos |
| Manutenção vs Operações desconectados | Conflitos de recursos |

**Risco:** Non-compliance com intervalos de manutenção → multas e acidentes.

### 3. A SOLUÇÃO: "Single Pane of Glass"

Conceito inspirado no **Google Earth**: uma única tela zoomável que transforma dados automaticamente baseado no nível de zoom.

| Zoom | Layer | Escala Temporal | Visualização |
|------|-------|-----------------|--------------|
| < 0.5x | **MACRO** | 10-20 anos | Heatmap (amarelo→laranja→vermelho) |
| 0.5-2x | **MESO** | 2-5 anos | Blocos DNA (retângulos coloridos) |
| > 2x | **MICRO** | 4-8 semanas | Cards detalhados com labels |

### 4. O 80/20 - O QUE IMPORTA PARA O DEMO

**80% do valor com 20% do esforço:**

1. **Visualização fluida a 60fps** com 50+ trens e 10 anos de dados
2. **3 níveis de LOD** que transicionam suavemente
3. **Indicadores de conflito** (vermelho) para chamar atenção
4. **Sidebar e timeline sincronizados** com a navegação

**O que NÃO precisa para o demo:**
- Integração real com Maximo
- Cálculos de custo
- Simulação What-If
- Drag-and-drop para reorganizar

### 5. TIPOS DE MANUTENÇÃO (Domínio)

| Nível | Intervalo | Duração | Custo Est. |
|-------|-----------|---------|------------|
| A-Check | 10.000 km | 4h | $2.500 |
| B-Check | 50.000 km | 16h | $8.500 |
| C-Check | 200.000 km | 72h | $45.000 |
| D-Overhaul | 800.000 km | 480h | $180.000 |

### 6. STACK TÉCNICO

| Camada | Tecnologia | Motivo |
|--------|------------|--------|
| Build | Vite 5.x | HMR rápido |
| UI Framework | React 18.3.1 | Requerido para BlueprintJS 6 |
| Componentes | BlueprintJS 6.1.0 | Enterprise-grade (Palantir) |
| 3D Engine | R3F 8.x + drei 9.x | React Three Fiber pareado com React 18 |
| Rendering | three 0.170.x | WebGL |

### 7. ESTADO ATUAL

**POC (canvas.html):** ✅ Validado - demonstra conceito completo
**Produção (apps/canvas):**
- F101-F103: ✅ Skeleton, R3F Canvas, BlueprintJS layout
- **F104:** ⬜ **PRÓXIMO** - Data types e mock generator
- F105-F117: ⬜ Pending

### 8. F104 - ESCOPO ESPECÍFICO

Portar do POC (`canvas.html`):

```typescript
// 1. CONFIG
const CONFIG = {
  TRAINS: 50,
  YEARS: 10,
  PX_PER_DAY: 10,
  PX_PER_TRAIN: 24,
  START_DATE: new Date(2024, 0, 1),
  // ...
};

// 2. Interfaces
interface Train { id, name, type }
interface Task { id, trainId, trainIndex, startDay, duration, type, color, conflict }

// 3. generateData() function
// - 50 trens
// - ~10 tasks/ano/trem = ~5000 tasks totais
// - 5% com flag conflict: true

// 4. Disponibilizar via Context ou store
```

### 9. DESAFIOS TÉCNICOS CHAVE

1. **Performance**: 60fps com 60k instâncias → InstancedMesh obrigatório
2. **LOD Transitions**: Sem flickering → estado consistente entre layers
3. **Sync Sidebar/Timeline**: Matemática precisa com `PX_PER_TRAIN * zoom`

### 10. REFERÊNCIAS DE INDÚSTRIA (Research)

| Metro | Abordagem | Resultado |
|-------|-----------|-----------|
| Hong Kong MTR | Genetic Algorithm | 99.9% on-time, +50% eficiência |
| Deutsche Bahn | Hypergraph Optimization | €74M/ano economia |
| Tokyo Metro | "Equalizing" | Workload nivelado |
| SNCF | Predictive Maintenance | 2.500 trens monitorados |

---

## ✅ PRONTO PARA IMPLEMENTAR F104

Entendi completamente:
- O contexto de negócio do cliente
- A necessidade de "Single Pane of Glass"
- A stack técnica (React 18 + R3F 8.x + BlueprintJS 6)
- O código do POC que precisa ser portado
- O estado atual da implementação (F101-F103 complete)

**Aguardo sua confirmação para prosseguir com a implementação de F104.**

Adicionei também a RFQ, que contem mais detalhes sobre o que o cliente precisará ver no canvas e também planejar no canvas (simulador what-if/sandbox).

1→# RFQ
     2→
     3→*Extracted from RFQ.pdf*
     4→
     5→---
     6→
     7→## Page 1
     8→
     9→ 
    10→RFQ Inputs 
    11→1.​ Scope of Work 
    12→Develop a solution that allows the maintenance and operations team to efficiently plan and schedule 
    13→preventative maintenance activities for two distinct rolling stock fleets, consisting of 49 and 79 trains 
    14→each (a total of 128 trains), while fulfilling operational requirements across the network. The system 
    15→should offer an automated, data-driven, and real-time mileage management approach, considering 
    16→different preventive maintenance activities, current resources, and operational needs. The requirements 
    17→can be divided into three main categories.  
    18→●​ Maintenance Planning 
    19→●​ Operational Planning 
    20→●​ Resource Planning  
    21→Maintenance Planning: Key requirement to meet the maintenance planning, 
    22→●​ Fetching daily mileage for each train from a specific location. 
    23→●​ Predicting maintenance schedules and dates based on real-time mileage data. 
    24→●​ Ability to adjust the maintenance schedule based on available resources (man-hours), train 
    25→availability, and maintenance tracks. 
    26→●​ The software should be capable of providing short-term (weekly, monthly) and long-term 
    27→(yearly 1-10) plans based on the fixed parameters (man-hours and tracks). 
    28→●​ Offer guidance and warnings on mileage underrun or overrun, as they can impact overall 
    29→mileage management. 
    30→●​ Availability to perform simulations on various planning and scheduling options. 
    31→ 
    32→Operation Planning: Key requirement to fulfil the operational planning 
    33→●​ Recommend the train numbers to be used daily based on the fitness status and mileage. 
    34→●​ Recommend the run assignment (mileage and the network) for each train. 
    35→●​ Provide warnings and recommendations to correct or adjust fleet mileage by comparing the 
    36→predicted mileage with the actual mileage. 
    37→●​ Provide an interface between the maintenance and operations teams to communicate on 
    38→train fitness and mileage management.  
    39→             Resource Planning: Key requirement to fulfil the Resource planning 
    40→●​ The resource planning should consider the manpower, spare costs and tracks available, 
    41→which are fixed parameters. 
    42→●​ The maintenance planning should consider the fixed parameters and avoid overutilization or 
    43→underutilization of the resources throughout the contract period (12 years) 
    44→●​ The software should be able to provide the maintenance costing for each fleet based on the

file.content:
# RFQ

*Extracted from RFQ.pdf*

---

## Page 1

 
RFQ Inputs 
1.​ Scope of Work 
Develop a solution that allows the maintenance and operations team to efficiently plan and schedule 
preventative maintenance activities for two distinct rolling stock fleets, consisting of 49 and 79 trains 
each (a total of 128 trains), while fulfilling operational requirements across the network. The system 
should offer an automated, data-driven, and real-time mileage management approach, considering 
different preventive maintenance activities, current resources, and operational needs. The requirements 
can be divided into three main categories.  
●​ Maintenance Planning 
●​ Operational Planning 
●​ Resource Planning  
Maintenance Planning: Key requirement to meet the maintenance planning, 
●​ Fetching daily mileage for each train from a specific location. 
●​ Predicting maintenance schedules and dates based on real-time mileage data. 
●​ Ability to adjust the maintenance schedule based on available resources (man-hours), train 
availability, and maintenance tracks. 
●​ The software should be capable of providing short-term (weekly, monthly) and long-term 
(yearly 1-10) plans based on the fixed parameters (man-hours and tracks). 
●​ Offer guidance and warnings on mileage underrun or overrun, as they can impact overall 
mileage management. 
●​ Availability to perform simulations on various planning and scheduling options. 
 
Operation Planning: Key requirement to fulfil the operational planning 
●​ Recommend the train numbers to be used daily based on the fitness status and mileage. 
●​ Recommend the run assignment (mileage and the network) for each train. 
●​ Provide warnings and recommendations to correct or adjust fleet mileage by comparing the 
predicted mileage with the actual mileage. 
●​ Provide an interface between the maintenance and operations teams to communicate on 
train fitness and mileage management.  
             Resource Planning: Key requirement to fulfil the Resource planning 
●​ The resource planning should consider the manpower, spare costs and tracks available, 
which are fixed parameters. 
●​ The maintenance planning should consider the fixed parameters and avoid overutilization or 
underutilization of the resources throughout the contract period (12 years) 
●​ The software should be able to provide the maintenance costing for each fleet based on the 
mileage and maintenance plan. 


## Page 2

●​ It should be able to provide different models or strategies in mileage management reflecting 
the changes in total maintenance costs in the short term and long term. 
2.​ Background 
●​
At present, no dedicated tools are available for mileage management. 
●​
The existing platform, IBM Maximo, primarily serves as an asset management tool and is 
not specifically for managing mileage. 
●​
Currently, mileage management is being managed manually through Excel. 
●​
Existing setup currently depends on raw mileage data that is stored and collected 
manually (folder driven mileage data), which requires validation and system integration. 
●​
Different fleet types and importantly Increasing fleet size necessitate a centralized, 
automated solution to ensure better scheduling and compliance. 
●​
Limited integration between maintenance and operations on mileage decisions. 
●​
Risk of non-compliance due to mileage underrun/overrun as it is performed manually. 
●​
Lack of predictive simulations to evaluate planning options. 
●​
Insufficient visibility of resources vs. actual utilization. 
●​
Manual handling of data from multiple sources resulting in delays and inefficiencies. 
 
3.​ Software’s core deliverables 
The Mileage Management shall be capable of integrating, processing, and utilizing inputs from 
maintenance, operations, and other required input, ensuring an automated, data-driven, and rule-based 
approach. 
●​
Daily Mileage Data 
●​
Maintenance Plans 
●​
Maintenance Resources: 
o​ Man-hours (fixed parameter). 
o​ Track allocation for maintenance activities. 
o​ Spare parts availability and baseline cost. 
●​
Fleet Identification: Train IDs and fleet classification (Kinki Sharyo / Alstom) 
●​
Fleet availability and Train Fitness Status 
●​
Mileage Balance Monitoring based on recommended run assignment. 
●​
Daily train deployment requirements across the network. 
●​
Adjustments between predicted vs. actual mileage utilization. 
●​
Maintenance budget forecasting 
 
4.​ Software input 
●​ Train IDs and fleet type (Kinki Sharyo and Alstom) 
●​ Daily Mileage – Live data 
●​ Daily train fitness status 
●​ Daily operation requirement and command for each train run IDs 
●​ Maintenance plans (e.g. PM, overhauls) 


## Page 3

●​ Track availability 
●​ Man-hours per maintenance 
●​ Maintenance baseline cost per maintenance – material cost, service cost 
●​ Man-hour cost 
●​ Base Depot for each fleet (Identification of the designated maintenance depot for each 
fleet) 
 
5.​ Software output 
●​ Centralised dashboard – This should provide one high level customised output and other 
comprises complete data, mileage status 
●​ Current maintenance status of all trains in terms of mileage 
●​ Train run assignments (Optimised allocation of trains for daily service based on fitness 
and mileage balance) 
●​ Completed and upcoming PM/overhaul activities (Predicted schedule of upcoming 
preventive maintenance and overhauls) 
●​ Maintenance compliance status (Real-time tracking of adherence to maintenance 
requirements based on run assignments) 
●​ Monthly and yearly cost compliance (Comparison of actual vs. planned maintenance 
costs on a monthly and yearly basis) 
●​ Standard and custom report generation (PDF, Excel) 
●​ Simulation report of different models on mileage management 
●​ Email notification of the generated reports to the stakeholders 
 
6.​ User Interface 
●​ Web-based application (preferred) accessible on desktops and mobile devices. 
●​ Capability to integrate with MMS (Maximo IBM) 
●​ Data security and compliance with IT standards. 
●​ Scalable architecture to accommodate additional fleets and extended functionalities. 
●​ Customizable alerts/notifications 
●​ Full accessibility as an admin. 
 
7.​ Implementation support 
●​ Project implementation methodology and timeline. 
●​ Case studies or references of similar implementations. 
●​ Supply, installation, and commissioning of the Mileage Control Management software. 
●​ Warranty and post-deployment support, including upgradation as per regular software 
updates and needs. 
●​ Migration of existing maintenance data into the system. 
●​ User training and knowledge transfer (Comprehensive training sessions for system users 
to ensure smooth adoption) 


## Page 4

●​ Documentation (Detailed technical and user manuals covering all functionalities) 
 
8.​ Case studies 
 Example:  Workload (Man-hour projection required vs available): 
 
a) Without adjustment: 
 
 
b) With adjustment 
 
 
9.​ Phase deployment 
 
●​ Phase 1 – with regards to complete requirements as outlines above, phase 1 should have 
implementation stage to cover the mileage management and run assignments. 
●​ Phase 2 – This phase where the costing part is included and required output is driven. 


## Page 5

●​ Phase 3 – Inclusion of room for the additional fleet into the system for which system 
retains the capabilities to do so. Competence Management part to be added to the 
system which can regulate the competency of the user. 
●​ Phase 3 – To be defined (Future scope)

From the RFQ, the client needs:
- Maintenance Planning (mileage-based scheduling)
- Operational Planning (run assignments, daily fleet utilization)
- Resource Planning (man-hours, tracks, costs)
- Simulations (what-if scenarios)
- Conflict detection (resource overutilization)
- Cost forecasting

The data structure must support ALL of these from day 1, even if we only visualize some initially.

Let me think about what a complete data model would look like...

message.content: Não é minha especialidade, avalie você, mas imagino que você deve pensar nos dados de uma forma que escalem mas também que permita diferentes analises, como em um sistema de analise de dados complexo (algo como open telemetry? apenas exemplo, não entendo), eu sei que existe também projetos o apache superset e outros, talvez possamos aprender com tecnicas que eles usam (não necessariamente fazer o mesmo, mas aprender e usar o que for melhor para nós)

content.thinking:
The user is asking me to think like a data architect. They're suggesting I look at how systems like OpenTelemetry, Apache Superset, and other data analysis platforms structure their data for:
1. Scalability
2. Multiple analysis perspectives
3. Flexibility

Let me think about this more deeply. The user wants me to research best practices and apply them to our domain.

Key insights from data engineering:

1. **Dimensional Modeling (Star Schema / Snowflake)**
   - Facts (events/measurements) vs Dimensions (context)
   - This is what powers BI tools like Superset, Tableau, etc.

2. **OpenTelemetry approach**
   - Traces, Spans, Metrics, Logs
   - Everything has timestamps, IDs, and attributes
   - Hierarchical relationships (trace → spans)

3. **Time-series optimizations**
   - Data bucketed by time periods
   - Pre-aggregations for different granularities

4. **OLAP Cubes concept**
   - Slice and dice by any dimension
   - Roll-up / drill-down capabilities

For our MMS system, let me think about what "facts" and "dimensions" we have:

**Facts (Events/Measurements):**
- Maintenance tasks (the core events)
- Mileage readings (daily snapshots)
- Resource utilization (daily aggregates)

**Dimensions (How to slice):**
- Time (year, quarter, month, week, day)
- Asset (individual train)
- Fleet (Alstom vs Kinki)
- Depot (Al Rashidiya vs Jebel Ali)
- Maintenance Type (A, B, C, D)
- Status (scheduled, completed, overdue)

This dimensional approach would allow:
- "Show me all D-overhauls for Alstom fleet in 2025"
- "Compare resource utilization between depots"
- "Aggregate costs by maintenance type by year"


I'll dive deeper into potential data modeling strategies, exploring how we can create a flexible and scalable architecture that meets our specific requirements.

Links: [{"title":"Dimensional modeling in Fabric Data Warehouse","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/fabric/data-warehouse/dimensional-modeling-overview"},{"title":"Kimball's Dimensional Data Modeling | The Analytics Setup Guidebook","url":"https://www.holistics.io/books/setup-analytics/kimball-s-dimensional-data-modeling/"},{"title":"Star schema - Wikipedia","url":"https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Star_schema"},{"title":"AI-Augmented Data Modeling: Enhancing Star Schema Design for Modern Analytics | American Scientific Research Journal for Engineering, Technology, and Sciences","url":"https://asrjetsjournal.org/American_Scientific_Journal/article/view/11930"},{"title":"The Star Schema: Making Your Data Warehouse Shine","url":"https://motherduck.com/learn-more/star-schema-data-warehouse-guide/"},{"title":"Understand star schema and the importance for Power BI - Power BI | Microsoft Learn","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/power-bi/guidance/star-schema"},{"title":"Data Warehouse Models & Modeling: Dimensional | Best Practices","url":"https://www.exasol.com/hub/data-warehouse/models-modeling/"},{"title":"Data Modeling Techniques you need to adopt in 2025 to master","url":"https://www.sparity.com/blogs/data-modeling-techniques-in-2024/"},{"title":"Data Modeling Best Practices For Data & Analytics Engineers","url":"https://www.thoughtspot.com/data-trends/data-modeling/data-modeling-best-practices-for-analytics-and-data-engineers"},{"title":"Mastering Dimensional Modeling: Strategy, Schemas & a 4-Step Design Process","url":"https://www.ewsolutions.com/mastering-dimensional-modeling/"}]

Here are the key findings on dimensional modeling, star schema, and time series data analytics best practices:

## Star Schema Fundamentals

The star schema is the simplest style of data mart schema and is the approach most widely used to develop data warehouses and dimensional data marts. It consists of one or more fact tables referencing any number of dimension tables.(no content)Star schema is a dimensional modeling design technique adopted by relational data warehouses and is a recommended design approach. A star schema comprises fact tables and dimension tables.

## Key Benefits of Star Schema

There are 5 key benefits of using a star schema: **Simplicity** - the design is simple and intuitive, easy to understand and work with. It has become the standard model for analytics tools. **Performance** - it's optimized for fast queries and data storage, critical when working with large data sets or complex aggregates.(no content)**BI Tool Compatibility**: Most BI tools (Tableau, Power BI, Looker, etc.) are designed with star schemas in mind. They can often automatically detect relationships between facts and dimensions, making drag-and-drop report building much easier.

## Time-Based Design Best Practices

One of the most valuable lessons in data engineering is the immense value of a dedicated DimDate table. Don't rely on using date functions directly on fact table columns in every query. Instead, pre-populate a DimDate table with all relevant date attributes (day of week, month name, fiscal periods, holidays, etc.). Create it once, populate it for a few decades (past and future), and use it everywhere.(no content)**Temporal data modeling** focuses on modeling data with a time aspect, capturing data changes over time. It enables tracking historical data changes and performing temporal queries, valuable for applications requiring historical data analysis and time-series databases.

## Slowly Changing Dimensions (SCDs)

An SCD is a dimension that tracks attribute changes over time. Type 1 overwrites history, Type 2 adds a new row per change (with effective dates), and Type 3 adds new columns for previous values.

## Modern Architecture Best Practices (2024-2025)

Use Data Vault or 3NF in the Silver layer when audit trails or flexible integration are required. Use Star/Snowflake in the Gold layer to simplify analytics. For small projects or proofs of concept, you can move from Bronze directly to Gold, but this sacrifices lineage and scalability.(no content)Modern analytics pipelines benefit from clear facts and dimensions: **Data quality & governance** through surrogate keys, SCD handling, and conformed dimensions ensure trust in enterprise metrics. **Performance at scale**: Columnar storage (e.g., Snowflake, BigQuery) plus star schemas deliver sub-second dashboards on billions of rows. **Semantic layer readiness**: Tools like dbt and Metrics Layer align naturally with well-defined dimensional models.

## AI-Augmented Design (Emerging Trend)

The star schema remains a foundational dimensional modeling approach in business intelligence, valued for its simplicity, performance, and OLAP compatibility. However, manual schema design is labor-intensive and error-prone in large-scale environments. Recent research shows that AI models like GPT-4, Google Gemini, and Meta's LLaMA 3 can be used for automating star schema generation, with AI-assisted modeling reducing development time by over 80%.

## When Not to Use Star Schema

Star schemas are optimized for read-heavy analytical workloads (OLAP), not for high-frequency transactional writes (OLTP). The structure and redundancy don't align with applications requiring rapid inserts and updates with strict normalization.

Here are the search results for Apache Superset data model design patterns for scalable analytics:

## Overview

Superset is lightweight and highly scalable, leveraging the power of your existing data infrastructure without requiring yet another ingestion layer.(no content)Apache Superset is a modern, enterprise-ready business intelligence web application that enables users to explore and visualize data through interactive dashboards. Originally developed by Airbnb and later donated to the Apache Software Foundation, Superset has become a popular choice for organizations seeking a flexible, scalable BI solution.

## Data Model Architecture

Apache Superset is based on a Dataset-Centric methodology, which lies firmly in the middle of a query-centric and semantic-centric architecture. This architecture promotes the use of datasets.(no content)Superset features a lightweight semantic layer that allows users to define custom dimensions and metrics. This enables business users to create consistent, reusable definitions for calculations and aggregations without modifying the underlying data.(no content)Datasets in Superset are your analytics building blocks. You can define: Physical datasets (point directly to a table or view) and Virtual datasets (define a SQL query that becomes a reusable dataset).

## Scalable Architecture Patterns

Superset is designed to support a large number of users and data sources, requiring a scalable architecture: Distributed Architecture using distributed databases, distributed queries, and distributed caching to scale Superset horizontally, supporting more data and concurrent users.(no content)Data Sharding: Implement data sharding techniques such as range sharding, hash sharding, and random sharding to distribute data across multiple nodes. Load Balancing: Utilize load balancing solutions like HAProxy and Nginx to distribute traffic evenly across servers, preventing bottlenecks.

## Real-Time Analytics Design

For real-time use cases, Superset uses Asynchronous Query Execution via Celery and Redis to run dashboard queries in the background, preventing the UI from freezing and allowing dozens of charts to load in parallel. It provides Advanced Caching with a configurable, multi-layer cache. You can set a short Time-To-Live (TTL) on charts to balance data freshness with database performance.(no content)By combining Snowflake's purpose-built interactive tables and warehouses with Superset's flexible front-end and caching, you create a powerful, scalable, and cost-effective stack for operational analytics. This architecture shifts the heavy lifting of data aggregation and real-time refresh to the data warehouse, allowing Superset to function as a highly responsive, high-concurrency visualization layer.

## Best Practices for Scalability

By following these patterns, you'll get the best of Apache Superset: fast exploration, trustworthy metrics, and analytics that scale with your business.(no content)For highly interactive analytics, consider columnar, OLAP-friendly engines. Superset + ClickHouse delivers blazing-fast aggregations.(no content)Create the interactive table that will back your dashboard. This table should be pre-aggregated and clustered for your most common query patterns.

## Core Components

The Superset application is comprised of the Python (Flask) backend application (server), API layer, and the React frontend, built via Webpack, and static assets needed for the application to work.(no content)Store the results of queries to your data warehouse so that when a chart is loaded twice, it pulls from the cache the second time, speeding up the application and reducing load on your data warehouse. Most people use Redis for their cache.(no content)Superset uses one or more workers who execute tasks like run async queries or take snapshots of reports and send emails, and a "beat" that acts as the scheduler and tells workers when to perform their tasks. Most installations use Celery for these components.

Slice and Dice With this it is possible to... | Download Scientific Diagram","url":"https://www.researchgate.net/figure/Drill-down-and-roll-up-d-Slice-and-Dice-With-this-it-is-possible-to-present-the-OLAP_fig3_221201012"},{"title":"Mastering OLAP: Slicing, Dicing, Drilling Down, and Rolling Up with Examples | by Ankur Aggarwal | Medium","url":"https://medium.com/@ankuraggarwa459/mastering-olap-slicing-dicing-drilling-down-and-rolling-up-with-examples-651d1e7a71e7"},{"title":"OLAP cube - Wikipedia","url":"https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/OLAP_cube"},{"title":"GitHub - feonit/olap-cube-js: olap cube javascript","url":"https://github.com/feonit/olap-cube-js"},{"title":"Implement OLAP cubes from facts and dimensions to slice, dice, roll-up, and drill down – DrVijayanand's Subjects","url":"https://drvijayanandonline.wordpress.com/2024/09/07/implement-olap-cubes-from-facts-and-dimensions-to-slice-dice-roll-up-and-drill-down/"},{"title":"OLAP Cubes Ming-Nu Lee","url":"https://www2.cs.sfu.ca/CourseCentral/454/johnwill/olap_cubes.pdf"}]

Here are the results for your search on OLAP cube operations and JavaScript implementation:

## OLAP Cube Operations Overview

Online analytical processing (OLAP) is a computer-based technique of analyzing data to look for insights. The term cube here refers to a multi-dimensional dataset, which is also sometimes called a hypercube if the number of dimensions is greater than three.

### Key Operations:

**Slice:** Slice performs a selection on one dimension of the given cube, thus resulting in a subcube.

**Dice:** The dice operation defines a subcube by performing a selection on two or more dimensions.

**Roll-up:** A roll-up involves summarizing the data along a dimension. The summarization rule might be an aggregate function, such as computing totals along a hierarchy.

**Drill-down:** Drill Down/Up allows the user to navigate among levels of data ranging from the most summarized (up) to the most detailed (down).

---

## JavaScript Implementations

### 1. **olap-in-memory** (GitHub: romain-gilliotte/olap-in-memory)
This library provides methods like `drillUp()`, `dice()`, `slice()`, and `project()` for querying cubes. Example usage includes:
- Unlike slice operations, dices can be chained on any given dimension.
- Drilling up a cube allows aggregating its data on any given dimension.

### 2. **olap-cube-js** (GitHub: feonit/olap-cube-js)
The project is on stage of developing API. As a frontend developer, the author created this tool to solve problems related to cubic data of a hierarchical type.

This library provides operations like:
- `cube.slice(member)` - for slice operations
- `cube.dice({ mark: markMember, region: regionMember })` - for dice operations
- `cube.getDimensionMembers()` - for accessing dimension members

---

## Implementation Approach

Implementing OLAP cubes involves several steps, including defining the structure with facts and dimensions, and then using operations like slice, dice, roll-up, and drill-down to analyze the data.

Key components:
- Facts are quantitative data points or measures you want to analyze. In an OLAP cube, these are typically aggregated. Example: Sales Amount, Quantity Sold.
- Dimensions are descriptive attributes by which the facts are analyzed. Hierarchies within dimensions help in organizing the data into different levels of granularity. Example: For the Time dimension, you might have Year > Quarter > Month > Day.

Here are the search results for OpenTelemetry data model spans, metrics, attributes, and dimensional analysis:

## OpenTelemetry Data Model Overview

The OpenTelemetry data model for metrics consists of a protocol specification and semantic conventions for delivery of pre-aggregated metric timeseries data. The data model is designed for importing data from existing systems and exporting data into existing systems, as well as to support internal OpenTelemetry use-cases for generating Metrics from streams of Spans or Logs.

### Spans

An OpenTelemetry span represents a single unit of work within a system. It encapsulates information about a specific operation, including its start time, duration, associated attributes, and any events or errors during its execution.(no content)Spans in OpenTelemetry track operations within your application and offer deep visibility into their execution. They are particularly valuable in distributed systems, where they can propagate across services to provide a complete view of how requests flow through different distributed components.

### Attributes

Attributes are keys and values that are applied as metadata to your spans and are useful for aggregating, filtering, and grouping traces.(no content)Attributes provide extra dimensions and context to your telemetry data, be it logs, traces, metrics, or events.(no content)Metric attributes follow the same naming conventions as spans. If attributes are specific to a metric, they should be namespaced under the metric. However, more general attributes can be used across multiple metrics.

### Dimensional Analysis and Transformations

OpenTelemetry has identified three kinds of semantics-preserving Metric data transformation that are useful in building metrics collection systems as ways of controlling cost, reliability, and resource allocation. The OpenTelemetry Metrics data model is designed to support these transformations both inside an SDK as the data originates, or as a reprocessing stage inside the OpenTelemetry collector. These transformations are: Temporal reaggregation (metrics collected at high-frequency can be re-aggregated into longer intervals), Spatial reaggregation (metrics produced with unwanted attributes can be re-aggregated into metrics having fewer attributes), and Delta-to-Cumulative (metrics with Delta temporality unburden the client from keeping high-cardinality state).

### Metric Cardinality

Metric cardinality is defined as the number of unique metric time series (MTS) produced by a combination of metric name and its associated dimensions. A metric has high cardinality when it has a high number of dimension keys, and a high number of possible unique values for those dimension keys.

### Converting Spans to Metrics

SpanConnector is a component in the OpenTelemetry Collector that allows you to derive metrics from span data. This is particularly useful when you have robust tracing but lack native metrics support in your language or framework. Converting traces to metrics offers valuable insights into system performance and health without requiring separate instrumentation.(no content)The connector pulls span data and aggregates them into Request, Error and Duration (R.E.D) OpenTelemetry metrics. Request counts are computed as the number of spans seen per unique set of dimensions, including Errors.(no content)Each additional dimension is defined with a name which is looked up in the span's collection of attributes or resource attributes (process tags) such as ip, host.name or region. If the named attribute is missing in the span, the optional provided default is used. If no default is provided, this dimension will be omitted from the metric.

content.thinking:
Excellent! Now I have a solid understanding of data modeling best practices. Let me synthesize this into a proposal for the MMS data architecture. The key insights are:

1. **Star Schema (Kimball)**: Facts (measurements) + Dimensions (context) - ideal for analytics
2. **OpenTelemetry**: Attributes for dimensional filtering, temporal aggregation
3. **OLAP operations**: Slice, dice, roll-up, drill-down for multi-dimensional analysis
4. **Superset approach**: Dataset-centric, semantic layer for reusable definitions

For MMS, I should design:
- **Fact tables**: Tasks (the events), Resource Usage (daily snapshots), Mileage readings
- **Dimension tables**: Time (DimDate), Asset, Fleet, Depot, MaintenanceType
- **Pre-computed aggregations**: For different time granularities (day, week, month, year)
- **Hierarchies**: Fleet → Asset, Year → Quarter → Month → Week → Day

This will allow:
- Slice by any dimension (show only Alstom, show only D-overhauls)
- Dice by multiple dimensions (Alstom + 2025 + D-overhauls)
- Roll-up (aggregate tasks by month, by quarter, by year)
- Drill-down (from yearly view → monthly → daily)

Let me now propose a complete data architecture.

Antes, me ajude com uma coisa, estou criando uma pesquisa/relatorio via chat do gemini, ele montou este o plano a seguir, analise e melhore-o, com base nos seus proprios aprendizados/analise: (1) Analisar as Imagens 1 e 2 para identificar como os tempos de tarefa são representados (ex: blocos de 8h) e verificar se há indicação visual de tempos de movimentação ou restrições de pátio.
(2) Pesquisar metodologias de planejamento de metrôs (JICA, UITP, Europa) e algoritmos de otimização (Resource Leveling, CSP), focando em como integram restrições físicas e logísticas.
(3) Investigar arquiteturas de dados de sistemas complexos: avaliar se modelos de BI (Superset) ou Monitoramento (DataDog) atendem a sistemas transacionais de agendamento, ou se são necessárias abordagens como CQRS e modelos baseados em grafos/tabelas de tempo.
(4) Realizar uma pesquisa específica sobre 'Depot Shunting/Switching Operations' e 'Yard Management' para entender o impacto do 'Tempo de Manobra' (movimentação entre estacionamento, oficina e lavador) na janela efetiva de manutenção.
(5) Buscar e compilar uma lista abrangente de 'Edge Cases' e modos de falha em agendamento ferroviário, como: 'Deadlocks' em trilhos de oficina, indisponibilidade não planejada de peças, falhas em equipamentos de via (tornos), e conflitos de competência técnica.
(6) Investigar como o 'Tempo de Manobra' afeta a disponibilidade da frota: pesquisar sobre gargalos de movimentação (ex: apenas uma locomotiva de manobra disponível) e como isso deve ser modelado como uma restrição dura no software.
(7) Sintetizar todas as descobertas no arquivo RESEARCH.md, estruturando-o em: Modelagem de Dados e Arquitetura, Algoritmos e Estratégias, e uma seção detalhada de 'Análise de Riscos e Edge Cases Operacionais' com foco em logística de pátio.

**screen-6.png**: Micro View (Operational 4-8 Weeks) - THE MOST DETAILED

### FASE 3: Edge Cases Categorizados (Risco × Impacto)

```
(3.1) CLASSIFICAR EDGE CASES POR CRITICIDADE

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ ALTA PROBABILIDADE + ALTO IMPACTO (CRÍTICOS)               │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ • Pit conflict (2 trens no mesmo pit)                      │
│ • Man-hours exceeded (capacity > 100%)                     │
│ • Dependency violation (tarefa B antes de A terminar)      │
│ • Overrun mileage (passou do intervalo + tolerância)       │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ BAIXA PROBABILIDADE + ALTO IMPACTO (CONTINGÊNCIA)          │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ • Wheel lathe breakdown (equipamento crítico falha)        │
│ • Shunting locomotive unavailable (não pode mover trens)   │
│ • Emergency maintenance (trem quebra em operação)          │
│ • Multiple trains cascade delay                            │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ ALTA PROBABILIDADE + BAIXO IMPACTO (MONITORIA)             │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ • Minor schedule slip (task atrasa 1-2h)                   │
│ • Spare part delay (peça chega 1 dia depois)              │
│ • Technician sick leave (recurso humano indisponível)      │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

(3.2) PARA CADA EDGE CASE CRÍTICO:

Documentar:
- Condição de detecção (como o sistema identifica?)
- Visualização (como aparece na UI?)
- Resolução automática (solver pode resolver?)
- Resolução manual (usuário precisa intervir?)
```

ACHADO: "Wheel lathe é recurso compartilhado crítico"
→ IMPLICAÇÃO: DimEquipment com tipo "shared_critical"
→ IMPLICAÇÃO: Constraint especial no solver

ACHADO: "Campaigns agrupam tasks relacionados"
→ IMPLICAÇÃO: Entidade Campaign com CampaignTasks
→ IMPLICAÇÃO: Arrastar campanha move todas as tasks


(4.2) VALIDAR MODELO CONTRA IMAGENS

content.text:
Entendi perfeitamente! Isso muda o contexto:

---

## 🎯 REFRAMING: O Verdadeiro Objetivo

**As imagens são VISÃO, não especificação.**

O objetivo real é:

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  "Reduzir em 80%+ o overhead cognitivo de análise e        │
│   planejamento de um sistema com infinitas possibilidades"  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**Como?** Através de:
- **Single Pane of Glass** - Tudo em uma tela, sem navegar entre páginas
- **Semantic Zoom** (Google Earth) - Contexto muda com o zoom, não a página
- **Game-like WebGL** - Saindo de charts tradicionais para visualização imersiva
- **Direct Manipulation** - Arrastar para reagendar, não preencher formulários

---

## 📊 PLANO DE PESQUISA REVISADO

Dado que as imagens são visão (não spec), o foco muda para:

### 1. PRINCÍPIOS DE DESIGN QUE REDUZEM OVERHEAD COGNITIVO

```
Pesquisar:

(1.1) "Information visualization cognitive load reduction techniques"
      → Como dashboards bem projetados reduzem tempo de análise?

(1.2) "Google Earth semantic zoom UX patterns"
      → O que faz o zoom do Google Earth ser tão intuitivo?

(1.3) "Direct manipulation vs form-based interfaces scheduling"
      → Por que arrastar é melhor que preencher formulário?

(1.4) "Game UI patterns for complex data enterprise"
      → O que podemos roubar de jogos de estratégia (Factorio, Cities Skylines)?

OBJETIVO: Documentar os PRINCÍPIOS, não copiar as imagens
```

### 2. VIABILIDADE TÉCNICA COM WebGL/R3F

```
Pesquisar:

(2.1) "React Three Fiber large dataset visualization 60fps"
      → Como renderizar 60k+ objetos mantendo performance?

(2.2) "InstancedMesh LOD switching strategies"
      → Qual a melhor forma de trocar entre Macro/Meso/Micro?

(2.3) "WebGL heatmap shader real-time aggregation"
      → Como gerar heatmap no shader sem reprocessar dados?

(2.4) "drei Html overlay performance large datasets"
      → Quando o HTML overlay (MicroLayer) fica lento?

OBJETIVO: Validar que a VISÃO é tecnicamente possível
```

### 3. DOMÍNIO: O QUE O CLIENTE REALMENTE PRECISA

```
Voltar ao RFQ e extrair:

(3.1) DECISÕES que o usuário precisa tomar:
      - "Qual trem usar amanhã?" (daily run assignment)
      - "Quando agendar próximo D-Overhaul?" (long-term planning)
      - "Estamos dentro do orçamento?" (cost compliance)

(3.2) INFORMAÇÕES necessárias para cada decisão:
      - Run assignment → mileage atual, fitness status, próximo serviço
      - Scheduling → capacidade depot, conflitos, dependencies
      - Budget → custo acumulado vs planejado

(3.3) FREQUÊNCIA de cada decisão:
      - Diária: run assignment
      - Semanal: ajustes de schedule
      - Mensal: revisão de compliance
      - Anual: planejamento estratégico

OBJETIVO: Garantir que visualização serve às DECISÕES reais
```

### 4. EDGE CASES QUE QUEBRAM A VISÃO

```
Identificar cenários onde "Single Pane of Glass" falha:

(4.1) "E se tiver 500 conflitos simultâneos?"
      → Heatmap de conflitos? Filtro? Priorização automática?

(4.2) "E se o usuário quiser comparar 3 cenários?"
      → Split view? Overlay com diff? Métricas side-by-side?

(4.3) "E se precisar de relatório para stakeholder externo?"
      → Export? Print view? Dashboard simplificado?

(4.4) "E se for acessar do celular?"
      → Responsive? Ou assumir desktop-only para demo?

OBJETIVO: Antecipar onde a visão precisa de exceções
```

### 5. SÍNTESE: DOCUMENTO DE DESIGN PRINCIPLES

```
Estrutura final (não é RESEARCH.md, é DESIGN_PRINCIPLES.md):

# MMS Design Principles

## 1. Core Vision
   - Single Pane of Glass
   - Semantic Zoom
   - Direct Manipulation
   - Game-like Visualization

## 2. Cognitive Load Reduction
   - Principle: Show don't tell
   - Principle: Progressive disclosure
   - Principle: Spatial memory over navigation
   - Principle: Color as data, not decoration

## 3. Technical Constraints
   - Performance budget: 60fps with 60k objects
   - LOD thresholds: Macro < 0.5x, Meso 0.5-2x, Micro > 2x
   - Data structure optimized for each view

## 4. Decision Support Mapping
   - Decision → Information needed → Visualization
   - Each zoom level serves different decisions

## 5. Known Limitations
   - Mobile: desktop-only for V1
   - Scenarios: max 2 comparison for V1
   - etc.
```

---

## 🤔 PERGUNTA PARA VOCÊ

Dado esse reframing, qual desses caminhos faz mais sentido agora?

**A) Pesquisa profunda** - Executar o plano acima, documentar tudo, depois implementar F104

**B) Implementar + Iterar** - Fazer F104 com a estrutura de dados que propus, e a pesquisa informa melhorias incrementais

**C) Híbrido** - Fazer pesquisa rápida (2-3 horas), documentar princípios essenciais, então implementar

Minha recomendação: **C) Híbrido** - porque:
- A visão já está clara (imagens + RFQ)
- A estrutura de dados que propus já suporta os requisitos
- Pesquisa muito longa atrasa o demo
- Podemos iterar baseado em feedback visual real

input.content:
# UI/UX Analysis - Reference Screens

> This document captures UI/UX insights extracted from the AI-generated reference images.
> These images represent the VISION, not a pixel-perfect specification.
> Use this as context for future development conversations.

---

## Core Vision

**Goal:** Reduce cognitive overhead by 80%+ in analyzing and planning a complex maintenance system with "infinite possibilities" (scheduling combinations).

**Inspiration:**
- Google Earth/Maps semantic zoom
- Game-like WebGL visualizations (Factorio, Cities Skylines)
- "Single Pane of Glass" - everything in one view, no page navigation

---

## Reference Screens Analysis

### screen-1.png: Simulation Setup Panel

**What it shows:**
- Heatmap in background (maintenance density)
- Modal dialog: "Simulation Setup: Scenario A"
- Constraints panel:
  - Labor Availability: 50% (slider)
  - Date Range: Nov 01 - Nov 15
  - Infrastructure: Maintenance Pit Status (toggle)
- "Run Solver (Est. 45s)" button

**Key UX Insights:**
- Solver is a background operation with time estimate
- Constraints are adjustable before running
- Infrastructure can be toggled (simulate pit unavailability)

**Implementation Notes:**
- Need a constraint configuration UI
- Solver should be async with progress indication
- "What-If" scenarios start with constraint changes

---

### screen-2.png: Full System Overview

**What it shows:**
- Header metrics: "Fleet Avail: 98%", "Annual Budget: On Track"
- Maintenance Heatmap spanning years (2021-2022+)
- Legend: Routine maintenance, Mid-life overhauls, Capacity bottlenecks
- Zoom popup: "Zoom in 4 weeks" showing OCT 2028
- Detailed tasks: "Brake Rev. T104 (8h)", "Wheel Lathe T112 (4h)"
- Dependencies shown as arrows
- Tooltip with: Density, Dependency info, Reserved slot, Technical use
- Time Scrubber at bottom

**Key UX Insights:**
- Global KPIs always visible in header
- Semantic zoom: click area → zoom to detail
- Dependencies are visual (arrows)
- Rich tooltips with scheduling metadata

**Implementation Notes:**
- Header should show real-time computed metrics
- Zoom can be triggered by click + area selection
- Dependency visualization is critical for understanding constraints

---

### screen-3.jpg: Multi-Scale View (All 3 LODs)

**What it shows:**
- **Macro 12-Year View "Heatmap"**: 2025-2037
  - Density clusters showing workload concentrations
  - Color intensity = task density

- **Meso 2-Year View "DNA Strip"**: Jun-Jan
  - Individual task blocks visible
  - Orange and blue colors for different types
  - Diagonal pattern (staggered scheduling)

- **Micro 4-Week View "Detailed Gantt"**: Oct 2028
  - Tasks with durations: "Brake Rev. T104 (8h)"
  - Tooltip: "Duration: 8h | Resources: 2 Techs, Pit 3 | Cost: $1200"

- Time Scrubber spans all views

**Key UX Insights:**
- Each LOD serves different planning horizons
- Macro → Strategic (years)
- Meso → Tactical (months)
- Micro → Operational (weeks/days)
- Same data, different aggregations

**Implementation Notes:**
- LOD transitions must be seamless (no loading, no flicker)
- Data structure must support efficient aggregation at all levels
- Time Scrubber provides orientation across zoom levels

---

### screen-4.png: Dashboard with Campaign View

**What it shows:**
- All 3 views stacked (Macro, Meso, Micro)
- Micro view with complex dependency arrows
- Task details: "Task Group: Bogie Overhaul Campaign | T145, T146, T147"
- Resources: "8 Techs, Pit 1 & 2, Lift Crane"
- **Conflict Alert: "Overlapping with Signaling Upgrade"**

**Key UX Insights:**
- Tasks can be grouped into "Campaigns" (related work across trains)
- Conflicts are explicitly called out with alerts
- Resource requirements include equipment (Lift Crane)

**Implementation Notes:**
- Campaign entity: groups tasks that must be planned together
- Conflict detection must consider: time, pits, equipment, staff
- Alert system for conflicts (not just visual indicators)

---

### screen-5.png: Meso View with Capacity Chart

**What it shows:**
- Train IDs: T001-T050 on Y axis
- Timeline: 2028-2029 on X axis
- Diagonal task pattern (resource leveling visible)
- **Bottom Panel: Capacity Utilization Chart**
  - Mechanical (blue bars)
  - Electrical (yellow bars)
  - Capacity Limit line
  - Time scale: 12 Year → 2028-2029 → etc.

**Key UX Insights:**
- Diagonal pattern = "Equalizing" methodology (Tokyo Metro style)
- Capacity split by work type (Mechanical vs Electrical)
- Capacity chart synced with main timeline
- Can see where capacity is exceeded (bars > line)

**Implementation Notes:**
- Capacity must be tracked by resource TYPE, not just total
- Resource leveling visualization is powerful for understanding spread
- Sync between main view and capacity chart is essential

---

### screen-6.png: Micro View with Pit Occupancy

**What it shows:**
- Header: "Fleet Avail: 99%", "Conflict Alerts: 0"
- Zoom Level: Micro (4 Weeks)
- Train rows: T001-T012
- Tasks: "T001: Brake Overhaul (8h)", "T002: Wheel Lathe (4h)", etc.
- **PIT OCCUPANCY Section:**
  - Pit 1, Pit 2, Pit 3, Wash Bay rows
  - Tasks shown in their allocated pit
  - **Red conflict indicator** on Pit 2 (overlap)
- Time scrubber: 1 Weeks | 2 Weeks | 4 Weeks | 6 Weeks

**Key UX Insights:**
- Pit allocation is a first-class visualization, not hidden data
- Conflicts visible at the resource level (not just task level)
- Wash Bay is separate resource type
- Time scale selector (1/2/4/6 weeks)

**Implementation Notes:**
- Pit Occupancy needs its own data structure (FactPitUsage?)
- Conflict indicator at resource level is critical
- Wash Bay may have different constraints (sequence requirement?)
- Flexible time window in Micro view

---

### screen-7.png: Scenario Diff View

**What it shows:**
- Title: "Advanced Gantt - Diff View: Scenario Comparison"
- Conflict zone: Red hatched area (Oct 1-3, T001-T002)
- Original task: Red border, in conflict zone
- Resolved task: Blue, moved to Oct 6
- Arrow showing the movement (conflict → resolution)
- Gray tasks: Unchanged

**Key UX Insights:**
- Scenario comparison is visual, not just metrics
- Conflicts shown as zones, not just task borders
- Resolution path shown explicitly (arrow)
- Color coding: Red=problem, Blue=solution, Gray=unchanged

**Implementation Notes:**
- Scenario diff needs: baseline tasks, modified tasks, conflict zones
- Visual diff is more powerful than table diff
- Animation of "resolution" could enhance understanding

---

## Design Principles Extracted

### 1. Progressive Disclosure via Zoom
- Don't show everything at once
- Zoom level determines information density
- Each level answers different questions

### 2. Spatial Memory over Navigation
- User learns where things are
- No page switches, just pan/zoom
- Context preserved during exploration

### 3. Conflicts are First-Class Citizens
- Not hidden in tooltips or logs
- Visual indicators at multiple levels
- Explicit alerts with descriptions

### 4. Resources are Visible
- Pit occupancy shown, not just calculated
- Capacity charts alongside schedule
- Equipment (Lift Crane, Wheel Lathe) tracked

### 5. Scenarios are Comparable
- Side-by-side or overlay comparison
- Diff view shows what changed
- Metrics delta (cost, conflicts, etc.)

---

## Technical Requirements Inferred

### Data Structure Needs
- [ ] Campaign grouping (tasks related across trains)
- [ ] Pit allocation tracking (temporal resource assignment)
- [ ] Equipment booking (Wheel Lathe, Lift Crane, Wash Bay)
- [ ] Scenario versioning (baseline + modifications)
- [ ] Capacity by type (Mechanical, Electrical)

### Visualization Needs
- [ ] Heatmap with configurable aggregation (day/week/month)
- [ ] Dependency arrows between tasks
- [ ] Conflict zones (not just task indicators)
- [ ] Capacity utilization chart synced with timeline
- [ ] Scenario diff overlay

### Interaction Needs
- [ ] Area selection → zoom
- [ ] Time scrubber with configurable range
- [ ] Constraint adjustment panel
- [ ] Solver trigger with progress
- [ ] Task drag-and-drop (future)

---

## Open Questions for Implementation

1. **Dependency arrows at Meso level?** - Images show them, but may clutter
2. **Capacity chart position?** - Bottom (screen-5) or side panel?
3. **Pit Occupancy always visible?** - Or toggle/tab?
4. **Campaign editing?** - Move whole campaign or individual tasks?
5. **Conflict resolution suggestions?** - Solver proposes, human accepts?

---

*Document created: 2025-12-15*
*Source: AI-generated reference images (screen-1 through screen-7)*
*Purpose: Context for future development conversations*

# UI/UX Analysis - Reference Screens

> This document captures UI/UX insights extracted from the AI-generated reference images.
> These images represent the VISION, not a pixel-perfect specification.
> Use this as context for future development conversations.

---

## Core Vision

**Goal:** Reduce cognitive overhead by 80%+ in analyzing and planning a complex maintenance system with "infinite possibilities" (scheduling combinations).

**Inspiration:**
- Google Earth/Maps semantic zoom
- Game-like WebGL visualizations (Factorio, Cities Skylines)
- "Single Pane of Glass" - everything in one view, no page navigation

---

## Reference Screens Analysis

### screen-1.png: Simulation Setup Panel

**What it shows:**
- Heatmap in background (maintenance density)
- Modal dialog: "Simulation Setup: Scenario A"
- Constraints panel:
  - Labor Availability: 50% (slider)
  - Date Range: Nov 01 - Nov 15
  - Infrastructure: Maintenance Pit Status (toggle)
- "Run Solver (Est. 45s)" button

**Key UX Insights:**
- Solver is a background operation with time estimate
- Constraints are adjustable before running
- Infrastructure can be toggled (simulate pit unavailability)

**Implementation Notes:**
- Need a constraint configuration UI
- Solver should be async with progress indication
- "What-If" scenarios start with constraint changes

---

### screen-2.png: Full System Overview

**What it shows:**
- Header metrics: "Fleet Avail: 98%", "Annual Budget: On Track"
- Maintenance Heatmap spanning years (2021-2022+)
- Legend: Routine maintenance, Mid-life overhauls, Capacity bottlenecks
- Zoom popup: "Zoom in 4 weeks" showing OCT 2028
- Detailed tasks: "Brake Rev. T104 (8h)", "Wheel Lathe T112 (4h)"
- Dependencies shown as arrows
- Tooltip with: Density, Dependency info, Reserved slot, Technical use
- Time Scrubber at bottom

**Key UX Insights:**
- Global KPIs always visible in header
- Semantic zoom: click area → zoom to detail
- Dependencies are visual (arrows)
- Rich tooltips with scheduling metadata

**Implementation Notes:**
- Header should show real-time computed metrics
- Zoom can be triggered by click + area selection
- Dependency visualization is critical for understanding constraints

---

### screen-3.jpg: Multi-Scale View (All 3 LODs)

**What it shows:**
- **Macro 12-Year View "Heatmap"**: 2025-2037
  - Density clusters showing workload concentrations
  - Color intensity = task density

- **Meso 2-Year View "DNA Strip"**: Jun-Jan
  - Individual task blocks visible
  - Orange and blue colors for different types
  - Diagonal pattern (staggered scheduling)

- **Micro 4-Week View "Detailed Gantt"**: Oct 2028
  - Tasks with durations: "Brake Rev. T104 (8h)"
  - Tooltip: "Duration: 8h | Resources: 2 Techs, Pit 3 | Cost: $1200"

- Time Scrubber spans all views

**Key UX Insights:**
- Each LOD serves different planning horizons
- Macro → Strategic (years)
- Meso → Tactical (months)
- Micro → Operational (weeks/days)
- Same data, different aggregations

**Implementation Notes:**
- LOD transitions must be seamless (no loading, no flicker)
- Data structure must support efficient aggregation at all levels
- Time Scrubber provides orientation across zoom levels

---

### screen-4.png: Dashboard with Campaign View

**What it shows:**
- All 3 views stacked (Macro, Meso, Micro)
- Micro view with complex dependency arrows
- Task details: "Task Group: Bogie Overhaul Campaign | T145, T146, T147"
- Resources: "8 Techs, Pit 1 & 2, Lift Crane"
- **Conflict Alert: "Overlapping with Signaling Upgrade"**

**Key UX Insights:**
- Tasks can be grouped into "Campaigns" (related work across trains)
- Conflicts are explicitly called out with alerts
- Resource requirements include equipment (Lift Crane)

**Implementation Notes:**
- Campaign entity: groups tasks that must be planned together
- Conflict detection must consider: time, pits, equipment, staff
- Alert system for conflicts (not just visual indicators)

---

### screen-5.png: Meso View with Capacity Chart

**What it shows:**
- Train IDs: T001-T050 on Y axis
- Timeline: 2028-2029 on X axis
- Diagonal task pattern (resource leveling visible)
- **Bottom Panel: Capacity Utilization Chart**
  - Mechanical (blue bars)
  - Electrical (yellow bars)
  - Capacity Limit line
  - Time scale: 12 Year → 2028-2029 → etc.

**Key UX Insights:**
- Diagonal pattern = "Equalizing" methodology (Tokyo Metro style)
- Capacity split by work type (Mechanical vs Electrical)
- Capacity chart synced with main timeline
- Can see where capacity is exceeded (bars > line)

**Implementation Notes:**
- Capacity must be tracked by resource TYPE, not just total
- Resource leveling visualization is powerful for understanding spread
- Sync between main view and capacity chart is essential

---

### screen-6.png: Micro View with Pit Occupancy

**What it shows:**
- Header: "Fleet Avail: 99%", "Conflict Alerts: 0"
- Zoom Level: Micro (4 Weeks)
- Train rows: T001-T012
- Tasks: "T001: Brake Overhaul (8h)", "T002: Wheel Lathe (4h)", etc.
